title: firewall rules are left behind, breaks networking from new pods using the port
body:
/kind bug **Description** Podman seems to occationally fail to remove port DNAT rules from iptables while pod is stopped. This causes double entries for the same DNAT ports while the same pod comes back as new one. But the older rules takes preceedence, leading all the incoming traffic being DNATted to old ip. Firewall is still iptables on this fedora 29. **Steps to reproduce the issue:** 1. I have systemd configured pod: ``` [Unit] Description=openhab Podman Container After=network.target [Service] Type=simple TimeoutStartSec=2 ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/podman rm openhab User=root ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman run --name openhab \ --rm -p 8083:8080/tcp -p 8101:8101/tcp -p 5007:5007/tcp -v "/var/lib/containers/exported_volumes/openhab_addons:/openhab/addons:Z" -v "/var/lib/containers/exported_volumes/openhab_conf:/openhab/conf:Z" -v "/var/lib/containers/exported_volumes/openhab_userdata:/openhab/userdata:Z" --hostname=openhab.ikenet --memory=512M -e EXTRA_JAVA_OPTS="-Duser.timezone=Europe/Helsinki" \ openhab/openhab:2.5.1-amd64-alpine ExecReload=-/usr/bin/podman stop "openhab" ExecReload=-/usr/bin/podman rm "openhab" ExecStop=-/usr/bin/podman stop "openhab" Restart=on-failure RestartSec=30 [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target ``` Point being, container with few ports forwarded, always started/stopped by systemd. 2. I do start and stop the container, and after while start it again. I've done it now some tenths of times, and this has happened twice in 24 hours. ``` systemctl start openhab-container-pod.service systemctl stop openhab-container-pod.service do config changes, and again: systemctl start openhab-container-pod.service ``` 3. access the port ``` # curl localhost:8083 curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 8083: Connection refused ``` **Describe the results you received:** ``` # curl localhost:8083 curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 8083: Connection refused ``` **Describe the results you expected:** a web page **Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):** These duplicates end up into iptables: ``` [ikke@ohuska ~]$ sudo iptables-save|grep '8083\|8101\|5007' -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -s 10.88.7.60/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8083 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8083 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8083 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.88.7.60:8080 -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -s 10.88.7.60/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8101 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8101 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8101 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.88.7.60:8101 -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -s 10.88.7.60/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 5007 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 5007 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 5007 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.88.7.60:5007 -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -s 10.88.7.61/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8083 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8083 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8083 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.88.7.61:8080 -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -s 10.88.7.61/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8101 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8101 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8101 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.88.7.61:8101 -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -s 10.88.7.61/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 5007 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 5007 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -A CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 5007 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.88.7.61:5007 -A CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT -p tcp -m comment --comment "dnat name: \"podman\" id: \"85f4a1c6a7dfb36c7f2900ee2916e7479978803f601a8c8382d3f2804007f152\"" -m multiport --dports 8083,8101,5007 -j CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 -A CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT -p tcp -m comment --comment "dnat name: \"podman\" id: \"26930a164f5af8f1ac58a15e4a9af2e1bc45aec8304a2f055a6f1226fe8ca10e\"" -m multiport --dports 8083,8101,5007 -j CNI-DN-439378ce6efde5c598da4 ``` I clean them up: ``` sudo iptables-save|grep '8083\|8101\|5007' sudo iptables --line-numbers --table nat -nL CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 9 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 8 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 7 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 6 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 5 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 4 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 3 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 2 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-DN-1172fb90efdf22f0fd4f1 1 sudo iptables --table nat -D CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT 10 ``` curl now works: ``` $ curl http://localhost:8083/index.html Error 404 Not Found

HTTP ERROR 404

Problem accessing /index.html. Reason: 

    Not Found

Powered by Jetty:// 9.4.20.v20190813
``` **Output of `podman version`:** ``` # podman version Version: 1.6.2 RemoteAPI Version: 1 Go Version: go1.11.13 OS/Arch: linux/amd64 ``` **Output of `podman info --debug`:** ``` debug: compiler: gc git commit: "" go version: go1.11.13 podman version: 1.6.2 host: BuildahVersion: 1.11.3 CgroupVersion: v1 Conmon: package: conmon-2.0.2-1.fc29.x86_64 path: /usr/bin/conmon version: 'conmon version 2.0.2, commit: ceeba0a4753c9599d6b136b0462de9f3bc7a8f68' Distribution: distribution: fedora version: "29" MemFree: 4062302208 MemTotal: 8039215104 OCIRuntime: name: runc package: runc-1.0.0-95.rc9.gitc1485a1.fc29.x86_64 path: /usr/bin/runc version: |- runc version 1.0.0-rc9+dev commit: 26f9c71f0ee5e621be1ae6d0aa4bfa1a8d072e4c spec: 1.0.1-dev SwapFree: 8199860224 SwapTotal: 8199860224 arch: amd64 cpus: 4 eventlogger: journald hostname: ohuska.localdomain kernel: 5.3.11-100.fc29.x86_64 os: linux rootless: false uptime: 7h 57m 31.46s (Approximately 0.29 days) registries: blocked: null insecure: null search: - docker.io - registry.fedoraproject.org - quay.io - registry.access.redhat.com - registry.centos.org store: ConfigFile: /etc/containers/storage.conf ContainerStore: number: 11 GraphDriverName: overlay GraphOptions: overlay.mountopt: nodev,metacopy=on GraphRoot: /var/lib/containers/storage GraphStatus: Backing Filesystem: extfs Native Overlay Diff: "false" Supports d_type: "true" Using metacopy: "true" ImageStore: number: 11 RunRoot: /var/run/containers/storage VolumePath: /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes ``` **Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):** ``` podman-1.6.2-2.fc29.x86_64 ``` **Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):** physical Fedora 29

comments.body: This makes me think, does this actually survive reboots? I rebooted today as I thought it could have fixed the problem, but the problem remained. So could it be such ghost rules are left into podman database and would get recreated at the next boot?
I don't *think* they should survive reboots - we do call into CNI immediately after reboot, but it's to tear down any networks that survived reboot, which should only be deleting rules.
(It's not strictly necessary to do that for IPTables, but we need it to clear other state - notably static IP allocations)
@vrothberg Poke - does that systemd unit look suspect? I'm wondering if the cleanup process is being killed before it can clean up the network

> @vrothberg Poke - does that systemd unit look suspect? I'm wondering if the cleanup process is being killed before it can clean up the network

That might be the case, especially as `KillMode=none` is not set but important. I suggest following the presented approach for creating systemd services in a recent blog post:
https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-shareable-systemd-services
Thanks, I added the killmode switch, let's see. I'll modify my ansibles to have the other additions too.

Hmm, I'm reading the blog, which is good, but one thing caught my eye. You have ```podman stop -t 10``` for timeout, but in the lower sample you have ```podman rm -f``` without any timeout. Woud it make sense to first stop it with something that has timeout, and then run the rm, just to make sure the rm doesn't take forever or being executed too early?

like:
```
ExecStop=/usr/bin/sh -c "/usr/bin/podman stop -t "{{ container_stop_timeout }}" `cat /%t/%n-cid`"
ExecStop=/usr/bin/sh -c "/usr/bin/podman rm -f `cat /%t/%n-cid`"
```

as the rm command doesn't seem to input timeout value.
I believe podman rm -f will send the stop signal and wait 10 seconds by default before killing the container.

ok, behaviour is just different. So if one wants to select the time for whatever reason, then separate stop first allows to do that.

I still had one occurrence of this problem even after adding the KillMode=none. I added now the pid and cid too from blog, and told the pid to systemd. Let's see if it helps.
@ikke-t I think you have a fix, if not reopen this issue.
